one_line_takeaway: A Graphormer + LLM framework that learns a spatio-semantic hazard graph to trigger safety-aware robot replanning, beating LTL and LLM-only baselines on hazard notice/handling in AI2-THOR, but it is a physical functional-safety paper with no adversarial threat model and small, LLM-labeled simulated data.
key_finding: On the risk-detection task, at a decision threshold of 0.21 the Graphormer achieves Recall 91.39% and Precision 29.27% under 1%-positive class imbalance (random guessing gives near-zero precision). On the LLM-guided planning task (Table II), the full LLM+Graphormer system dominates all baselines on safety awareness while preserving task success: Safety Notice Rate (SNR) is 100% at every complexity level, and Risk Handling Success (RHS) is 100/95/95% for simple/intermediate/complex, versus SNR 0% and RHS 0/0/10% for LLM-Only and 0% SNR for safety-prompted LLM. The LLM+LTL baseline also reaches 100% SNR but its RHS degrades sharply with complexity (90/80/40%) and its Task Success Rate drops to 60% on complex tasks, whereas Ours keeps TSR at 100/97.5/92.5%. The authors note LTL only works because they manually predefined rules covering all hazards, which they argue is infeasible at real-world scale.
core_contribution: The paper proposes GGTP, a Graphormer-enhanced, risk-aware task-planning framework for embodied/household robots that couples an LLM planner with a learned graph model of physical hazards. Its own contributions are: (1) a dynamic spatio-semantic 'safety graph' G=(V,E) whose nodes are environmental entities (objects, humans, robots) and whose edges carry LLM-annotated pairwise risk levels combined with spatial proximity; (2) a Graphormer model trained (with focal loss) to predict high-risk edges from this graph; and (3) a runtime replanning loop where a detected high-risk edge triggers the LLM to regenerate a safety-aware task sequence T'=LLM(T,G). The stated aim is to move 'beyond static rules' (LTL) toward adaptive, context-aware hazard perception in continuous environments. This is a functional-safety / physical-hazard-avoidance contribution (e.g., a child approaching a hot stove or a knife), NOT an adversarial AI-security contribution.
threat_model: No adversarial threat model. The paper addresses accidental physical hazards in the robot's environment (e.g., a child standing near a knife or hot kettle), not attackers, prompt injection, data poisoning, model extraction, or any malicious actor. 'Safety' here means IEC-61508-style functional safety and hazard avoidance, not security. No confidentiality/integrity/availability property is defended, and no adversary is modeled. Forcing a security frame onto this paper would misrepresent it, so none is asserted.
limitations: Grounded in the paper's own text and tables: (1) Risk ground truth is itself LLM-generated (r=LLM(vi,vj)) and then used to train and evaluate the Graphormer, a potential circularity the paper does not address. (2) Tiny, synthetic evaluation: 120 AI2-THOR scenes split 90/15/15, with the planning study run on only the first 20 kitchen scenes and 5 hand-defined tasks. (3) Precision is low (~29%): in the paper's own worked example, 300 edges are flagged to catch 90 of 100 true hazards, i.e. many false alarms. (4) The LTL timing comparison in Table III uses only ONE rule (Receive Safety Notice 0.0002s for LTL vs 1.1926s for Graphormer), which the authors concede is unrealistic since real deployments need thousands of rules, so the latency comparison is not apples-to-apples. (5) Risk perception is static/spatial only; temporal hazard prediction is left to future work. (6) Everything is simulation-only in AI2-THOR with a scripted 'HandleSafetyIssue' proxy; no real-robot or real-world validation.

datasets_used: Synthetic risk-aware dataset built in AI2-THOR: 120 diverse household environments across kitchens, living rooms, bedrooms, bathrooms; human-agent nodes injected near hazards in half the scenes; split 90 train / 15 validation / 15 test
Hazardous edges constitute only ~1% of all edges (severe class imbalance)

Planning evaluation subset: first 20 AI2-THOR kitchen scenes; 5 household activities across 3 complexity levels (2 simple spatial-reorganization, 2 intermediate object-manipulation, 1 complex cooking)
quantitative_results: Risk detection at threshold=0.21: Recall 91.39%, Precision 29.27%

Table III latency (Ours vs LTL, seconds): Receive Safety Notice 1.1926 vs 0.0002 (1 rule); Generate Task Sequence 1.4581 vs 0.9931; Build Environment Graph 0.0185 vs n/a; Retrieve Object Info 0.1552 vs 0.1257
baselines_compared: Rule-Based System (risk detection) - manual, non-exhaustive rules
Prompted LLM / LLM-Safe-Prompting (risk-aware prompting, objects only, no environment guidelines)
LLM + Static Rules (LTL) with manually defined rules

Related-work comparison table (Table I) cites AiGem, SMART-LLM, Plug in the Safety Chip, SafePlanner, RoCo, Co-NavGPT, TidyBot, AutoRL, Cross-Layer Sequence Supervision as prior systems (not run as baselines)
